title: 2025/10/15 - GA Subcommittee A (1 of 2)
displayTitle: 2025-10-15 - Governance and Administration Committee - Public Service Amendment Bill [Subcommittee A]
description: Wednesday, 15th October 2025 | Governance and Administration Subcommittee A Public Service Amendment Bill Hearing of evidence: Rights Aotearoa Paul Thistoll, CEO Trust Democracy Simon Wright, Chair Professor Jonathan Boston Interested Citizens Group - Hamilton Margaret Evans, Former Hamilton Mayor Raymond Mudford, Member Colin Jones, Member Russelle Knaap, Member Te Uru Tangata Centre for Workplace Inclusion Maretha Smit, Chief Executive Animal and Plant Health Association New Zealand Liz Shackleton, Chief Executive Jacob Sorenson
descriptionSummary: Public Service Amendment Bill. Rights Aotearoa. Paul Thistoll, CEO

chunks.text: Thank you. Welcome, Welcome. So we've got 10 minutes, uh, set aside for your submission. So you're welcome to use all of that, or, um, leave some time for Questions. I use about eight minutes on it, So, okay. Okay. And if you, Thanks. Good foreshadowing.

Thank you so much. Over to you Koto, be and honorable committee members. I'm Paul Theto, CEO of Rights. Rah. I appear before you today to deliver what I hope will be the most important testimony. Who you here on this bill? I contend I'm uniquely qualified to comment on the proposed legislation because I'm both a human rights professional and the holder of a master's degree in business strategy, organizational science and leadership from LBS of the business school.
The proposed legislation engages issues that the intersection of human rights and organizational strategy.

I was very proud of being accepted into LVS. It was an extremely competitive entry process, and competing the full-time taught degree on site in London was one of the most intense work periods of my life. Now, I appreciate this diversion because I had tremendous talks from world leaders, business leaders, and two sports coaches, Eddie Jones and the leader of the Ride Cup team, Paul McGinley.

And I'm gonna come back to them at the end about what makes a high performing team. So now I sit back before you in A-T-R-R-A Kiwi commenting on a proposed bill, which in my opinion represents one of the most catastrophically evidence defined pieces of legislation we've ever proposed. So I focus on the provisions of the bill, which will get rid of diversity, equity, and inclusion.

The first and overarching reason that we should reject the diversity, equity, and inclusion destroying provisions of this bill is that they're based on an entirely false premise. The bill's proponents claim diversity undermines merit. This is demonstrably empirically, categorically false. I will now present extremely strong research from London Business School that demonstrates the exact opposite.

So, first of all, I had a wonderful professor called Professor Nia Abara, who's a world leader in DEI. She has spent 30 years documenting what she calls second generation bias, the invisible barriers that prevent genuine meritocracy. Who research published in the Harvard Business Review incited over 15,000 times reveals that without DEI frameworks, organizations don't achieve merit-based selection.

They achieve homogeneity through unconscious exclusion. Old white men exclude minorities unconsciously through patterns of behavior. This is very clear throughout all her research. Consider her findings. Senior leaders unconsciously sponsor proteges who remind them of themselves.

Now, I know you will all have, we've all worked in organizations and you'll know, you'll see that pattern. Importantly, women receive 75% high, fewer high visibility assignments, critical for advancement performance criteria systematically favor stereotypically masculine traits.

And without structured DEI interventions, informal networks exclude diverse talent. So this isn't just me with a non-work ideology here. This is real research backed up from a leading business school. Now, I want to ask you to not close your eyes for a second, but just imagine all the leadership training you have ever been to in your life.

It has told you to be authentic, hasn't it? To work on some sort of authentic version of yourself and your leadership. Well, Nia has this amazing concept of the authenticity paradox, and it's particularly damning for this bill. So when marginalized people are told to just be yourself and workplaces designed for others, they face an impossible choice, conform and lose your, and lose your identity, or remain authentic and be excluded.

DEI frameworks resolve this paradox by broadening what leadership looks like. Removing these frameworks as the bill does, doesn't enhance merits. It restores the old boys network. We end up with a public sector that looks like the Vienna Philharmonic, not the New York Philharmonic.

And famously the New York Philharmonic is a very diverse orchestra where the output represents very well the output of global American and music schools, while the Vienna based orchestra looks more like our Prime minister. So my second professor

Was Professor ais nu, and he looked at how DEI helped organizations improve their performance. His 18 year longitudinal study published in management science tracked companies that adopted comprehensive ESG principles of which DEI is a critical component.

The results very impressive. High sustainability companies showed 5% higher annual returns, superior returns on assets and equity, and more importantly, an applicable to the public sector, public service, better operational efficiency and greater resilience during economic downturns.

So let's be clear, organizations with strong DEI frameworks outperform those who don't. Now, in my opinion, the bill's proposal to eliminate DEI is equivalent to a The other question. No, you continue. He wants to be the first question after you finish.

Okay, cool. Uh, so in the private sector, if a private sector company did this, I believe it would be considered managerial malpractice and that all the directors could be sued for breach of fiduciary duty. Now, luckily, we actually have some evidence about what will happen if you get rid of the DEI clauses in the bill.

And we have this from the United States Federal government earlier this year where they did something similar. So as I put in my written submission, and these are all referenced, there was a 23% resignation rate amongst employees of color. Within three months, 78% of terminated DEI positions were women of color.

There were service delivery failures, and there's ongoing constitutional crisis and litigation all the way to the Supreme Court. We don't want to re repeat this disaster in New Zealand. We actually have evidence from another jurisdiction. So coming briefly to the New Zealand context,

this bill would breach out to tour obligations requiring active protection of Maori interests, would violate our human rights treaties. We ratified, including CDO and I-C-C-P-R, potentially trigger mass re resignations, costing hundreds of millions, create legal chaos and destroy public service capability.

The only government department in favor of this bill will be Crown Law because they will all be, they will all have jobs for a very long time. Now, I notice I've gone over time a bit, so I'll just make two more points. I would conservatively estimate the economic cost of this bill to be over $4 billion in the first five years of its implementation.

Now, how do I get to such a large number? Well, after subtracting direct transfers like Welfare and Super Ann and cost of debt financing, the total public sector OPEX spend is around 89 billion New Zealand dollars a year. Imagine we lose just 2% of productivity and efficiency on that number and we have 3% more staff turnover.

We have some service delivery failures, some litigation costs. You easily get to that number as well as being qualified in business. I'm a human rights professional and I just want to say that I, to Toko the Human Rights Commission's excellent written submission, and I deeply support Dr. Pachecos oral submission.

This bill is both an organizational disaster area and a human rights nightmare. So, coming to the end, what would Eddie Jones and Paul McGinley, the Rider Cup captain who gave incredible talks. Now LBS was confidential, so I can't repeat the date on what they said, but I know from their public comments what they would've said.

Their approach to building high performance teams is categorically diverse because there is always high performing individuals. You'll always find high performing individuals on the streets, but building them into a team is the hard part. And interestingly, Eddie Jones has said publicly that psychological safety is the most important thing to building a team.

And so did Paul McGinley. And you get psychological safety from everyone feeling welcome and you get that from DEI and just, I didn't mention this, but Google has done incredible work on what makes high performing teams and organizations and they also identified psychological safety.

So honorable committee members, I contend that DDEI is the winning formula for both rugby teams, the Ryder Cup and the public service. Cool, Thank you so much. We had actually, um, used all your time now and I imagine, I imagine Ed Andy was not gonna do a short question knowing him. So thank you so much, uh, for joining us. Answer pushed. I can give you a very short answer.

No, that's fine. I apologies for running, no for running outta time. But thank you so much, um, for your presentation for joining us and alright, we've got our next submitters now trust democracy who are joining us. And so we've got Simon. Yeah, welcome. Thank you. Good morning seeing the drill. We've got 10 minutes for your oral submission today, so you're welcome to use all the time or our leave some time for questions over to you.

Okay. Um, thank you for having me. Uh, just a little bit about trust, democracy. Uh, it's a nonpartisan, um, nonprofit incorporated society, um, fairly new formed in 2019. And our purpose is to foster democratic innovation, uh, for a fair, just, and inclusive society.

Um, we actually support aspects of the, uh, bill, um, uh, especially around, um, the intention to improve performance review systems and frameworks. However, overall we see the bill is, um, regressive for democracy in New Zealand. Um, it further centralizes power, uh, weakens provisions that ensure the public service reflects the communities it serves, um, um, weakens mechanism mechanisms that help governments stay cognizant of, of, um, future opportunities and challenges.

And it increases the risks of politicization of public service. Um, the public services are cornerstone of the New Zealand system of government, and the laws governing it are some semi constitutional in nature. Uh, therefore, uh, this amendment should have been developed through an open and inclusive and and transparent process.

Um, unfortunately, the process used to develop this bill has not upheld those standards. Uh, policy work occurred largely in the public service without public participation and consultation has only occurred at this late stage committee. Um, that approach does not uphold the public services acts.

The public service acts own principles, particularly the principle of open government in section 12, and the process to develop a bill that reshapes the law governing the public service should itself uphold its own acts principles. So we recommend that the committee note these process deficiencies in its report parliament, and that future reforms follow the principle set out in the act.

Open government, um, has a 40 year history in New Zealand, um, yet still undefined in in law. Um, the development of this bill reinforces the need to add a clear statutory definition, anchored in access to information, public participation and accountability. Uh, defining open government in this way would make section twelve's principles more concrete and ensure that future reforms embody openness as a matter of course, and enable their practical use and integrity standards and in performance review frameworks.
And that's, that's the, the value add that they can, that they can bring.

Uh, trust democracy is concerned that several provisions, uh, centralized power in the offices of the Public Service commissioner and the chief executive, the department of, um, prime Minister and cabinet, uh, weakening safeguards against politicization Clause 30 gives the commissioner veto power over so-called key positions.

The criteria for these positions are extremely broad and not limited to senior leadership roles. Uh, um, reducing the independence of chief executives to make appointments makes political appointments easier. We recommend that clause 30 be removed or at a minimum that key positions be defined narrowly as senior executive roles with published criteria.

Clause 32 would allow fixed term employees to be appointed to DPM C'S Policy Advisory Group, which provides advice directly to the Prime Minister. This could enable political appointments to that group, undermining the neutrality of the public service. We therefore recommend that clause 34 B removed high standards of integrity and conduct are necessary for maintaining, uh, trust in the public service.

We support, um, making mandatory for the Public Service Commissioner to issue minimum standards of conduct and integrity, but Bill does not go far enough. Under Clause 14, the commissioner may decide whether these standards include matters relating to the public service principles and values.

We believe this should be mandatory. Uh, integrity standards must explicitly reflect the core values and principles defined in the act. Surely the current guidance, uh, as far as we can tell, was last updated in 2010. This is totally out of date and public, so that needs to be, um, uh, rectified, uh, urgently.

Um, we're also concerned about clause 15, which would extend, extend the commissioner's authority, authority to agencies across the wider state services, including the education sector that would further centralized control and could constrain the independence of institutions such as schools and universities to set their own standards.

We therefore recommend that integrity standards be required to cover the public service principles and values and that clause 15 be reconsidered. Uh, the bill removes provisions to ensure, uh, diversity, equity, and inclusion across the public service. Uh, we strongly object to this.

Um, these provisions were designed to ensure the public service reflect the society it serves and that employment decisions are fair and equitable. Uh, clauses 21, 34, 35 and 39 together remove or dilute, dilute obligations on chief executives and commissioner to promote diversity, inclusion and pay equity.

These are not minor adjustments. They reverse progress towards a fair and representative public service. This runs counter to OECD guidance, which stresses that diversity and inclusion must be maintained in public institutions because, uh, excluding underrepresented groups creates blind spots in policy design, decision making and service delivery.

We therefore urge the committee to reject these amendments and retain the current provisions safeguarding diversity, equity, and inclusion. The bill would centralize long-term insights briefings with DPMC. That's the wrong direction. Each department should continue to prepare its own briefings, engaging the public on long-term challenges and opportunities in its sector.

Centralizing foresight reduces diversity of thinking and weakens opportunities for meaningful engagements. To conclude the bill contains some positive elements, um, but its overall direction is concerning it. Centralizes power reduces inclusiveness and risks politicizing the public service, which undermines a longstanding tradition in this country. It's, Thank you so much. We do have some, uh, questions in a little bit of time. So Andy Foster.

Yeah, look, um, thanks so much Simon. Nice to see you again. Um, couple of areas of questions. First one of them is just in terms of the appointments to, uh, the, um, DPMC, et cetera. Yep. Uh, and I, I've, I take the point about political appointments, but sometimes when the public service, and you also talk about ideological capture of public service, um, the, there will be an argument that says the public services to a degree sometimes is ideologically captured by its own ideology.

And also, um, uh, the, the public service culture is, is sometimes ve to bureaucratic rather than public service. 'cause those are two very different things sometimes do you not need some people who are outside that public service in those roles to shake things up a little bit? And I I do look at, um, you know, the, the potentially bring in some private sector advice that, um, might be helpful.

Any comment on that? So it's say political, but it is about saying you want, sometimes you want people outside the public service to actually stir things up a little bit. Okay. I, I certainly, di diversity is part of your protection against the ideological capture, which is what I've argued in the, in the, um, those provisions around employment of public servants.

But I think there are many other ways of including To, Uh, you have political advisors in your offices. Um, there are many agencies have working groups made up of external people from across the New Zealand society experts. So I think there are plenty of opportunities without, um, uh, uh, affecting this particular institution.

The Okay. So it's, it's where you put 'em in the institution. Yeah. Um, and second area I wanted to ask you about was, you talked about, um, which I completely agree with, the performance reviews of chief executives. Do you think we are strong enough in terms of the KPIs and what we, what we look at as success from entities themselves? So I know that, that they're aligned with the, the, the chief executives Yeah.

But that we're clear enough about what success looks like for any given government agency. Look, I, I'm not an expert in that area at all. At all. Um, I, so I, I, I feel I I'm not really good to comment on that, although it would seem to me that things like principles and values set up and act, um, if those principles and values are well defined, they should be part of, of how agencies are assessed and the performance of chief executives are assessed. So we Should have expectations as the key point Or there are letters of expectations. Yes. Yeah.

I've just got a quick question, uh, too, so you mentioned where, um, I think in relation to, uh, clause 15, which means section 19 of the act, uh, that extends, I think you said, the guidance on integrity and conduct to, um, agencies that could include schools and universities. That is concerning to me.

'cause of course, uh, our tertiary institutions have academic freedom. So is is something that you would recommend? I know that I think you recommended removing that entirely, but I suppose another, um, improvement would be ex, specifically excluding as Crown Research Institutes are We, we, we trust democracy's a voluntary organization and most of us have day jobs, and we really struggled to understand how the, the new provisions in the bill.

Uh, so what, which groups of, um, what parts of the public service would be affected by those We suspected they, they, they could affect the education sector, but honestly, we found it so difficult to work out. So some clarity around that. No, that's, that would be really great, Really helpful, um, suggestion for us to take forward. Yeah. Thank you. We have run out of time now. Okay.

Um, but thank you so much for, for joining us and, and appreciate also to the previous submitter, um, the early morning start. Um, we really appreciate you being here, so thank you so much. Thank for that. And we've got our next submitter, uh, joining us by Zoom, um, professor Jonathan Boston. Welcome, Jonathan. Alright. We've only got five minutes for your submission, uh, so we'll, um, I'll let you know when that starts, but, um, please feel free to use that entire time.

We do have your detailed written submission with, um, uh, you know, recommendations involved as well. So, uh, over to you. Well, Moana, thank you very much. Just to say I agree with the two previous, uh, submitters. Uh, in the brief time I have, I just like to focus in on the proposed changes to the purpose statement.

Uh, as I'm sure members of the committee will be aware, the legislation design and advisory committee, uh, has highlighted that purpose statements really matter. They have legal weight, they help clarify the main purpose of an act. They signal the reasons for any change in high level policy. They provide the basis for, or at least one of the basis for implementing, monitoring and assessing the performance of an act.

And they provide guidance to lawyers and judges in their interpretation of the legislation. And as I noted in my submission, I was one of the primary drafters of the purpose statement in the current Public Service Act, along with Dr. Andrew Butler, kc. The current purpose statement in Section 11 encapsulates the critical and enduring constitutional roles of our public service.

It anchors the public service's legitimacy in values that extend beyond individual political cycles and underpins the need for prudent stewardship and administrative continuity across successive governments. The statement reads, the public service supports constitutional and democratic government enables both the current government and successive governments to develop and implement their policies.

Delivers high quality and efficient public services, supports the government to pursue the long-term public interest, facilitates active citizenship and acts in accordance with the law. The proposed amendments to this legislation remove three key provisions in the purpose statement.

First, enabling successive governments to develop and implement their policies. Second, supporting the government to pursue the long-term public interest and third, facilitating active citizenship. Well, I just have a simple question for the members of the committee. Why are these important roles which are generally agreed constitutionally being removed from the current purpose statement?

Could it possibly be the case that the government doesn't believe that the public service should enable successive governments to develop their policies? Or that the government doesn't believe that the public service should support governments to pursue the long-term public interest, or that the government doesn't believe that the public service should facilitate active citizenship?
So it's a question to the committee.

Thank you, Jonathan. Um, uh, we do have some questions for you and we've got a couple of minutes. I'll go to Andy Foster first. Yeah, look, thank you Jonathan. Um, just given that you've, uh, focused on the purpose statement, I'll do the same. Um, just in terms of the, um, the changes which were proposed, the one bit that in effect is added, there is the words and acts with the spirit of service to the community, um, which you could actually gently drop into the existing one, uh, ahead of, and acts in accordance with the law.

'cause that's in there as well. Um, do you, could you give us a a view on that, because it does strike me that there is a big difference between a bureaucracy which can tend to act in its own interests or what it believes should happen and service to the community? So being a public servant and being a bureaucrat are two somewhat different things in terms of their nature.

So Andy, the current legislation provides for the public service to act in a spirit of service. So the additional provision there, it doesn't change anything substantially what the change is, the removal of three important. I Got that. I've got that. But Jonathan, I'm asking you the question why, because you stressed the importance of the purpose statement.

Mm. Is you said the legislation includes that. So is there anything that is offensive to you in including with a Ann Acts with the spirit of service to the community in what is, of course, section 11? Uh, of course not Andy, but it's there in the legislation already. Fine. That's course, that's all I needed. Alright, thanks. Um, and thank you for your, uh, detailed, uh, written submission.

I just wanted to ask, you've said that you, uh, basically don't think this act should, should go ahead. Um, is there anything, uh, in this that would stand out apart from the purpose statement as the, as the main area that you think should be changed? Well, Well look, as my written submission indicates, I have grave reservations about a number of the provisions in the amending legislation.

And perhaps the most significant, frankly, are the changes to the appointment arrangements with respect to departmental chief executives, which I fear will increase the incentives for politicization, which would be devastatingly bad for a small parliamentary democracy like New Zealand. Uh, the, the probably the most fundamental point I'd want to make though, is that constitutional legislation of this kind should be developed on a cross party basis.

And in this case, it has not been, uh, the previous government made every effort to secure the support of parties outside the government. Um, sadly that wasn't completely successful, almost successful, but it did make the effort, the current government has made no such effort. Thank you. Thank you so much, Jonathan.

We've run out of time, but appreciate you being with us today. Thank you. Thank You. Alright, we have our next, uh, submitter, uh, group, uh, which is the Interested Citizen group, which is joining us via Zoom. Yeah. Yep.

Thank you. Thank you very much for joining us. Uh, we've got a copy of your written submission and we do have 10 minutes set aside for your oral submission today. So welcome to the committee. You're welcome to use all of that time. Uh, we'll leave some time for questions at the end. Uh, over to you. Thank you, Madam Chair. And, uh, I'm Margaret Evans and I'm a long-term mayor of Hamilton, and I think it's wonderful that we've got so much local government experience around your table.

And it's interesting that we follow up, Jonathan. Uh, I think that, um, I'm going to introduce the members with me today. Uh, on my side here is Raymond, who's got experience in, uh, international Affairs finance. This is Ruelle who comes from a background of, uh, commerce and marketing.

And then there's, uh, uh, um, I'm, I'm sorry. We are a very loose knit group. Now. This is an experience of finance real estate and developed, and I'm gonna get right to the point. This is really simple. Anybody that watched the results from last weekend's, local government, uh, uh, elections will understand the deep concerns about democracy in this country.

So we are simply saying, most people think it's the officials who run the place. It is called the bureaucracy. And we want to see elected representation strengthened. So we are saying to you, please put an addendum to this bill, send it across to the local government regulatory bill that's going through its process to ensure that the expectations of public servants, those across local government as well as central government.

So that's the message as simple as that. And I'm going to ask our team if they want to add anything else. Okay. Oh, that's Rayford here. Um, the all I've got really is a question as to how a group such as us could actually help progress the changes that you're promoting.

Um, Yes, I, I would agree with Margaret and also Ray. Um, I'm really concerned about the performance of city council in Hamilton. Um, I'm over the national policy statement in urban development capacity. Um, council has confirmed they're not complying with the legislation, and yet there seems to be no mechanism to actually hold anyone to account.

And, and this comes back to the point of who is actually running councils. And that's why we are so interested in the work that you are doing. And we see its relevance to local government. Michelle. Oh, I think that's, um, quite obvious in the low turnout at city council elections, you're lucky to get 20, 25% because most people feel that they can't, even if they vote, they're not influencing anything because there's a whole lot of other people behind the scenes that they dunno that are really in charge.

And quite often that is actually the case. So I guess that's our overview. We are saying you understand local government possibly as well as anybody. And we think that the concerns about public sector are widespread and we constantly hear the expression Yes, minister.

Simple. Thank you. Uh, well, thank you very much, uh, for your submission and your, um, and your ideas, um, that I think are, are, um, some different ideas for the ones that we've heard before. We do have some questions. I've got one and we'll start with Andy. Yeah. Look, um, look, thank you very much for the, the submission. Uh, yes, ministers were a favorite program, if I might say this still has great currency.

I think, um, the question is, we've got some comments that you've been made about the, um, the, the, the key power obviously sits in with, with chief executives in terms of the organizations, um, about the appointment of chief executives. And actually, uh, you've seen, you've referenced local government. We also have the same, uh, discussion in front of, um, US in terms of local government where the idea is to go from five plus two to five plus five or up to five.

And I just wonder if, um, you, and then here we've got the issue of do chief executives, they get to the end of five years and they've gotta reapply, uh, as opposed to potentially saying, actually you're doing a good job, carry on. Do you have a view as the best way of, um, appointing or reappointing extending or not extending chief executives both in the public sector, government, public sector, and in the public sector, local government sector?

Um, I'm happy to point out that the concern the local government level is not different from some of the concerns of central government about managerialism and about career people who go from one department to another department and end up in a senior position without real depth of knowledge which relates to that particular section.

And of course, the logical consequence that adds to the cost of consultants. So coming back to the point, uh, we've had some discussions about the proposed legislation to do with the ability to fire anybody over the salary peak of $180,000. And we think that's the side of the debate that needs further enhancing the ability to actually get rid of a non-performing chief executive without having the cumbersome problem of are they meeting all of these key points.

Uh, um, we do know that there are examples everywhere across the country of non-performing senior managers, but it's how you get rid of them. Okay, Thank you. If I could comment, there's also the, the question regarding the performance of those who are elected as well in that, uh, you know, certain promises are made invariably not carried out as they are, uh, integrated into the, the system.

And the, the conversation that we have here is the one using the recall provision, which is one that's used in the states. It's a legal process, but allows the community to actually say, enough is enough. We need to take some action, and here is the proper process for doing that.

And can I add to that? I think it's what Jonathan just said. We think it's timely in this country to have a cross party deep and extensive discussion about the problems that we have with democracy. And that includes the public sector. That leads us, of course, into the whole concept of constitutional discussion.

And if that's to be the case, we are saying do it broadly. Do it right across the country, do it deeply, allow opportunities for a range of people to talk. That's why we call ourselves the Concerned Citizens Group because we are not a formal organization. We just bring in people when we know that there's an issue that needs to be addressed.

And if you look deeply, you will see we've actually given submissions to three or four different aspects that have come through in the last month. And, um, I mean, that includes codes of conduct. We could start talking about that if you really wanted us to. I make a comment. Yeah. Thank you so much.

Well, yeah, we do do have another local government bill in front of us as well. Um, but no, we'll take those, um, those points on board and yeah, thank you for your, for your engagement and for your, um, uh, for your interesting suggestions to us. And, and we have, we have noted those. And thanks sofa, your engagement with other committees too. Appreciate that. Thank you. Thank you.

We've now got our next, um, submitter on, uh, in person, um, for workplace inclusion. Thank you. Thank you so much for joining us, uh, in person. We've received a copy of your written submission, um, and I believe we've got 10 minutes for your, um, oral submission.

So are you welcome to use time or leave some time for questions over to You. Sure. Thank you very much for having me this morning. Um, the organization that I represent today is Center for Workplace Inclusion. Um, you will probably best knowed as the previous Equal Employment Opportunity Trust. Yes. Um, so I just wanna offer a few reflections today.

Um, and I, I'm not going into detail in my, in my submission, you've got that there. Um, but the reason why I've chosen to be here today is because I'm genuinely worried, um, that in this time of global polarization around the terms diversity, equity, inclusion, we are about to throw out some of the most sensible clauses that help us build social cohesion, help us build, um, productivity and help us build good economic outcomes for.

So I wanna recognize, and, and our scope of submission was particularly around four clauses that speak to the diversity inclusion aspects and pay equity, um, specifically. Um, there are, there's much more in that act that we actually do support in terms of strengthening the public service. But in terms of those four clauses, um, Apologies.

I wanna recognize that there are groups in society, as there always is on the edges, um, who push for specific agendas. And unfortunately what we've seen over the last while is that those agendas was co-opted the terms and the disciplines of diversity, equity, inclusion, which really is all about how do we remove barriers for people to participate more productively in society.

How do we not look at just our small group of people in our networks when we make appointments, um, and when we make recommendations. So for me, the main issue today was, um, are we seeing that the definitions around diversity, equity, inclusion, the definitions of what ma what makes merit?

Are we seeing that those are actually impacting what we are proposing in this specific act? So, um, I wanted to specifically talk about merit. The, the bill claims to strengthen merit based appointments. Um, merit though is not neutral. Um, it is shaped by who gets seen, um, and who gets supported and who get a, who gets access.

So if we don't have any safeguards in our act, um, against the bias and against those excl exclusion, merit will just reinforce who we already have in our systems. So removing diversity, equity, inclusion from our, um, policy doesn't level the playing field.

Um, actually quite the opposite. It makes it more difficult for people who may actually have a lot of merit in what they want to offer, but it makes more difficult for them to participate. So my concern is that the problem with the ACT was not the clauses in and of themselves, but that there might have been overreach in terms of implementation.

The ACT have never stated any targets, never stated any quotas. Um, it simply requests that, that we consider the diversity and the equity and the inclusion in our society that speaks to social cohesion, that speaks to trust building. Yeah, I would like to, um, offer a very personal reflection.

And that's the fact that I come from South Africa. No, no doubt. You can hear that from the accent. Um, that's a society where exclusion was embedded in policy, that was a moral failure of that country. However, I have the lived experience of what it looks like when people can't see themselves in policy, where people feel excluded from policy.

Um, that is not just a system that disadvantages those excluded.
